APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work fits squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference: it tackles open‑domain QA using Wikipedia, introduces a scalable retriever + neural reader pipeline, and evaluates on standard benchmarks (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies) with appropriate metrics (EM/F1). The methodology and comparisons to strong baselines (Wikipedia Search API, YodaQA, BiDAF, DCN, MPM) match community expectations, and the positioning and related work coverage are thorough, making it relevant to the venue’s audience. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles an important open-domain QA problem with a practical, deployable pipeline (TF-IDF+bigram hashed retriever + multilayer BiLSTM reader) and demonstrates strong empirical gains (outperforming Wikipedia Search API and achieving state-of-the-art on SQuAD). The methodology, datasets (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies), baselines, and evaluation (EM/F1) match community expectations, and the paper emphasizes scalability and multi-task/distant-supervision training that are highly relevant to applied NLP researchers.

reviewer_a.justification: This work fits squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference: it tackles open‑domain QA using Wikipedia, introduces a scalable retriever + neural reader pipeline, and evaluates on standard benchmarks (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies) with appropriate metrics (EM/F1). The methodology and comparisons to strong baselines (Wikipedia Search API, YodaQA, BiDAF, DCN, MPM) match community expectations, and the positioning and related work coverage are thorough, making it relevant to the venue’s audience.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles an important open-domain QA problem with a practical, deployable pipeline (TF-IDF+bigram hashed retriever + multilayer BiLSTM reader) and demonstrates strong empirical gains (outperforming Wikipedia Search API and achieving state-of-the-art on SQuAD). The methodology, datasets (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies), baselines, and evaluation (EM/F1) match community expectations, and the paper emphasizes scalability and multi-task/distant-supervision training that are highly relevant to applied NLP researchers.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (Wikipedia-as-knowledge-source, TF-IDF+bigram retriever, and multi-layer Document Reader). Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported; figures, tables, and examples are informative and related work coverage is thorough. Writing quality is generally good, though the presentation of the multitask/distant-supervision experiments and the exact interplay between retriever vs. reader could be clarified further for a reader seeking precise reproduction details. | B(4): The paper is well organized and presents a clear logical flow from motivation to the DrWiki pipeline and experiments (intro → method → experiments → conclusion). Main contributions are stated early (Wikipedia-as-knowledge, TF-IDF+bigram retriever, multi-layer Document Reader, multitask/distant supervision) and experiments compare to standard baselines (BiDAF, DCN, etc.) with standard metrics (EM/F1). Notation and methodological details (including hyperparameters) are reported, and the writing quality is described as good, though a few minor presentation improvements (e.g., explicit code release statement or slightly deeper visual explanation of the retrieval/reader interaction) could make replication even clearer.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (Wikipedia-as-knowledge-source, TF-IDF+bigram retriever, and multi-layer Document Reader). Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported; figures, tables, and examples are informative and related work coverage is thorough. Writing quality is generally good, though the presentation of the multitask/distant-supervision experiments and the exact interplay between retriever vs. reader could be clarified further for a reader seeking precise reproduction details.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and presents a clear logical flow from motivation to the DrWiki pipeline and experiments (intro → method → experiments → conclusion). Main contributions are stated early (Wikipedia-as-knowledge, TF-IDF+bigram retriever, multi-layer Document Reader, multitask/distant supervision) and experiments compare to standard baselines (BiDAF, DCN, etc.) with standard metrics (EM/F1). Notation and methodological details (including hyperparameters) are reported, and the writing quality is described as good, though a few minor presentation improvements (e.g., explicit code release statement or slightly deeper visual explanation of the retrieval/reader interaction) could make replication even clearer.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main empirical claims (a TF–IDF + bigram-hash Document Retriever beating the Wikipedia Search API and a multi-layer biLSTM Document Reader obtaining strong SQuAD performance) are supported by experiments across multiple QA benchmarks, and methodology and hyperparameters are reported. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and none are required for the proposed pipeline. However, from the provided summary I note a lack of principled analysis or rigorous ablations that isolate the relative contributions of the retriever versus the reader (a requirement I weigh heavily), and no formal guarantees are given for the retrieval component. Overall the experimental methodology appears sound with reasonable baselines and datasets, so the conclusions are largely justified despite these theoretical and ablation gaps. | B(4): The paper clearly states and empirically supports its main claims: an efficient TF-IDF+bigram-hash Document Retriever plus a 3-layer biLSTM Document Reader yields strong open‑domain QA results (retriever outperforms Wikipedia Search API; reader attains SQuAD-level performance) and multitask/distant supervision helps scale to open‑domain datasets (CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies). The experimental methodology uses appropriate baselines (ElasticSearch API, YodaQA, BiDAF, DCN, etc.) and standard metrics (EM/F1), and hyperparameters are reported, which lends credibility for replication and deployment. Minor gaps include no mention of released code or statistical significance testing and limited analysis of cross‑dataset transfer failure modes, but these do not undermine the core technical claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main empirical claims (a TF–IDF + bigram-hash Document Retriever beating the Wikipedia Search API and a multi-layer biLSTM Document Reader obtaining strong SQuAD performance) are supported by experiments across multiple QA benchmarks, and methodology and hyperparameters are reported. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and none are required for the proposed pipeline. However, from the provided summary I note a lack of principled analysis or rigorous ablations that isolate the relative contributions of the retriever versus the reader (a requirement I weigh heavily), and no formal guarantees are given for the retrieval component. Overall the experimental methodology appears sound with reasonable baselines and datasets, so the conclusions are largely justified despite these theoretical and ablation gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states and empirically supports its main claims: an efficient TF-IDF+bigram-hash Document Retriever plus a 3-layer biLSTM Document Reader yields strong open‑domain QA results (retriever outperforms Wikipedia Search API; reader attains SQuAD-level performance) and multitask/distant supervision helps scale to open‑domain datasets (CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies). The experimental methodology uses appropriate baselines (ElasticSearch API, YodaQA, BiDAF, DCN, etc.) and standard metrics (EM/F1), and hyperparameters are reported, which lends credibility for replication and deployment. Minor gaps include no mention of released code or statistical significance testing and limited analysis of cross‑dataset transfer failure modes, but these do not undermine the core technical claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper’s main contributions are an effective engineering integration of existing retrieval and reader techniques and strong empirical results rather than a novel algorithmic breakthrough. Given the lack of new theoretical ideas or fundamentally new methods, but acknowledging the useful framing, retrieval augmentation, and solid empirical gains, a moderate originality score of 3 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a practical pipeline (TF-IDF + bigram-hash Document Retriever paired with a multi-layer biLSTM Document Reader) and a useful framing of using Wikipedia as a single open-domain knowledge source, but the core components are largely combinations of existing techniques rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic idea. The authors do clearly differentiate their work from prior QA systems and demonstrate empirical gains (e.g., retriever beating Wikipedia Search API and strong SQuAD reader performance), yet there is little theoretical analysis or novel methodological extension beyond integrating known retrievers and readers. From a theory‑purist perspective this reads as an engineering advance with moderate novelty rather than a significant conceptual breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable, practically-oriented contribution by framing open-domain QA over Wikipedia as a single-source, end-to-end pipeline and by combining an efficient TF–IDF + bigram-hash Document Retriever with a multi-layer RNN Document Reader trained on SQuAD and distantly supervised data. While the individual components (inverted-index retrieval, neural span extraction, multitask/distant supervision) are not themselves novel, the engineering integration, the specific retrieval augmentation (bigram hashing) and the demonstration of strong empirical gains (reader SOTA on SQuAD and improved open-domain QA performance) clearly differentiate it from prior work. From an applied-systems perspective this is a meaningful and informative advance rather than a purely incremental tweak.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a practical, high‑impact contribution by providing a scalable, reproducible retrieval+reader pipeline over Wikipedia that sets a strong baseline likely to be widely adopted. Reviewer A’s caution about limited theoretical novelty and ablation depth is valid, but these concerns do not diminish the paper’s clear applied impact and immediate utility to the community. Given the system-level advance, reproducibility, and demonstrated gains across benchmarks, a top impact score is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses an important, practical problem—open‑domain QA over Wikipedia—and presents a reproducible, high‑impact pipeline that outperforms the Wikipedia Search API and achieves SQuAD‑level reader performance while demonstrating multitask/distant‑supervision benefits on multiple benchmarks (CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies). From my perspective as a theory purist, the work is impactful because it operationalizes a clear, scalable paradigm (fast TF‑IDF + bigram hashing retriever + neural span reader) that other researchers and practitioners will adopt. However, I must downgrade from transformative because the contribution is primarily an empirical/engineering integration with no theoretical analysis or formal guarantees and limited ablation isolating retrieval vs. reader effects, which hampers deeper scientific generalization.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles the central, high-impact problem of open-domain QA over a single, realistic knowledge source (Wikipedia) and presents a practical, scalable pipeline (fast TF-IDF + bigram-hash Document Retriever + multi-layer biLSTM Document Reader) that outperforms off-the-shelf Wikipedia search and achieves state-of-the-art machine reading results on SQuAD. From an applied-systems perspective the combination of an efficient retriever, strong neural reader, and effective distant supervision techniques materially advances deployable QA systems, setting a new practical baseline that I expect researchers and practitioners will build upon widely and that opens clear directions for improving retrieval, transfer, and end-to-end training.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical evaluation is broad, compares to strong baselines, and includes useful engineering details, making the experimental contribution solid and practical. They also concur that the paper lacks thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses and full reproducibility (e.g., released code), which prevents a top score. Given the overall strengths but these notable gaps, a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides reasonably comprehensive empirical evaluation across multiple open-domain benchmarks (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies) and compares against sensible baselines (Wikipedia Search API, YodaQA, BiDAF, etc.), and reports hyperparameters. However, from the summary there is little evidence of rigorous ablations or sensitivity analyses that isolate the contributions of the TF-IDF/bigram retriever versus the neural Document Reader (beyond top-line comparisons), and no theoretical analysis or formal justification for the pipeline design — weaknesses I penalize as a theory-focused reviewer. Related work coverage appears thorough and some limitations are discussed, but reproducibility is only partial (code not mentioned), so the experimental depth is adequate but missing key analyses.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are broad and pragmatic: they evaluate both retriever and reader components, compare to strong baselines (Wikipedia Search API, YodaQA, several neural readers), report SOTA on SQuAD, and test transfer across multiple open-domain datasets. The paper includes sensible engineering details (bigram-hashed TF–IDF retriever, 3-layer biLSTM reader) and hyperparameters, and discusses limitations of using Wikipedia and task transfer — though the summary does not explicitly describe thorough ablation studies of design choices (e.g., hashing, number of retrieved docs) or provide released code for full reproducibility. Overall the empirical evaluation is solid and practical, with minor gaps in component-wise sensitivity analyses and public artefacts.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper conducts strong, fair empirical comparisons against sensible baselines and competitive readers across multiple datasets using standard metrics, and that related work is appropriately discussed. Reviewer A correctly notes minor missing analyses (deeper retrieval vs reader ablations and very recent systems) that prevent a perfect score, so I assign a 4 to reflect comprehensive benchmarking with some room for additional targeted evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a range of sensible baselines (Wikipedia Search API/ElasticSearch, YodaQA) and competitive neural readers (BiDAF, Dynamic Coattention, Multi-Perspective Matching) using the same metrics (EM/F1) across multiple datasets (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies). From a theory-purist perspective the empirical comparisons are generally fair, but the paper could do more to isolate retrieval vs reader contributions with targeted ablations and to include any very recent open-domain QA systems for completeness. It does acknowledge when prior methods are competitive and discusses related work, so overall the empirical comparison is strong with only minor omissions in analysis depth.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against strong and recent baselines across components (Wikipedia Search API, ElasticSearch, YodaQA) and competitive readers (BiDAF, Dynamic Coattention, Multi-Perspective Matching), using the same datasets (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies) and standard EM/F1 metrics. Comparisons appear fair and component-wise (retriever vs search API, reader vs state-of-the-art on SQuAD) and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably or pose limitations; related work is discussed thoroughly. From a systems perspective this constitutes comprehensive and appropriate empirical benchmarking for an applied open-domain QA pipeline.

main_claims: Proposes tackling open-domain question answering using Wikipedia as the unique knowledge source where answers are text spans in articles.
Introduces a pipeline (DrWiki) combining a fast TF-IDF + bigram-hashing Document Retriever with a multi-layer recurrent neural Document Reader for extracting answer spans.

Shows that the Document Retriever outperforms the built-in Wikipedia Search API and the Document Reader achieves state-of-the-art results on SQuAD; multitask learning with distant supervision is effective for open-domain QA.
_summary.methodology: Use an efficient inverted-index TF-IDF retriever augmented with hashed bigram features to return top-5 Wikipedia articles, then apply a 3-layer biLSTM Document Reader (with aligned question embeddings and manual features) trained on SQuAD and distantly-supervised examples to extract answer spans.
limitations: Wikipedia is designed for humans, not machines, which poses challenges for machine reading.
Task transfer is challenging: training on one dataset and testing on another can lead to poor performance (noted in related multitask work).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - open-domain question answering / machine comprehension

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in machine learning theory and formal methods with experience reviewing for NeurIPS and ICML.
traits: Penalizes papers that present primarily empirical/engineering improvements without theoretical analysis or formal guarantees (score penalty if no theory/ablation explaining why design choices work).
Demands rigorous ablations isolating contributions of major pipeline components (will deduct points if no quantitative breakdown showing retrieval vs reader effects).
Requires evaluation on diverse and challenging benchmarks beyond a single dataset (will lower score if only SQuAD and limited distant supervision are used).
Prefers novel algorithmic ideas over feature engineering or combination of well-known components (downgrades papers that are mainly an engineering integration of existing methods).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Skeptical, focused on formal understanding and clear attribution of empirical gains; will penalize papers that read as engineering systems papers without principled analysis.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Systems Engineer
reviewer_b.background: Principal researcher in applied NLP and information retrieval who builds deployable QA systems and has published engineering-heavy papers at ACL/EMNLP.
traits: Rewards practical, scalable systems work that demonstrates strong empirical performance and efficiency (boosts score if retrieval/latency/throughput improvements are shown).
Values use of real-world, single-source knowledge (Wikipedia) and end-to-end pipeline design that enables replication and deployment (gives credit for clear pipeline and reproducibility).

Favors papers that achieve state-of-the-art on established benchmarks and that effectively leverage distant supervision to scale (will raise score for convincing SQuAD/Opendomain gains and DS experiments).
Prefers comprehensive comparisons to standard baselines (will penalize only if obvious baselines are missing, but tolerant of engineering re-use).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and results-oriented: emphasizes empirical gains, scalability, and applicability; more forgiving of lack of theory when the system advances practical capabilities.
